text: This video shows how near-infrared laparoscopic fluorescence can guide fascial plane and nodal identification during total mesorectal excision for rectal cancer.
The first case shows the technique and technology in an elderly patient with low rectal cancer.
Fluorescent areas are visible at the base of the mesocolon, indicating the location of lymph nodes along the superior rectal artery.
The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection of the mesocolon, focusing on the major arterial axis, as indicated by the fluorescent guidance for lanodissection.

The surgeon uses near-infrared laparoscopic fluorescence to guide the proximal arterial transection, ensuring all relevant nodal tissue is included within the specimen during total mesorectal excision.
The surgeon injects three milliliters of indocyanine green submucosally beneath the rectal cancer using a proctoscope, preparing for definitive operative resection.
The surgeon utilizes the Pinpoint laparoscopic system from Novadact Technologies during the procedure, providing enhanced visualization of anatomical structures.
This technology can be used as a normal high-definition white light camera, but also as a near-infrared energy source and detection system.
combine the images seen here in the upper middle left part of the screen so that an augmented reality real-time view can be provided during the entire operation.
The main screen image can be switched between white light near infrared and a combination view by switch activation.

The surgeon utilizes the Pinpoint laparoscopic system to visualize important lymphatic channels and nodes during the operation, enhancing visibility of these structures along embryological fascial planes.
Indocyanin green is a non-selective fluorophore and so gives no indication as to whether these nodes contain cancer cells.
It is simply indicating that these nodes and channels are within the delta drainage of the primary cancer.
The next case demonstrates a procedure in a sixty-two-year-old patient again with rectal cancer and again undergoing total mesorectal excision.
Again, you can appreciate the clarity by which we can visualise lymph nodes during the case.
In this instance, the major medial lymphatic uptake, however, relates to retroperitoneal lymph nodes.
The surgeon identifies the correct plane for medial to lateral dissection, preparing to separate tissue layers in the rectal cancer excision procedure.
as in the previous case.
The surgeon performs posterior and lateral dissection of the mesorectum, utilizing NIR fluorescence to visually enhance the color coding of lymphatic channels and lymph nodes.
The surgeon mobilizes the mesorectum forward, revealing specific green fluorescent points that correspond to mesorectal lymph nodes, visible due to NIR fluorescence.
The surgeon identifies secondary lymphatic uptake within the retroperitoneum, carefully distinguishing between the visceral and parietal fascia during dissection.
During the ongoing dissection, lymphatic channels draining the rectum become increasingly visible, along with specific fluorescent points representing lymph nodes within the mesorectum.
Aside from assisting the operating team,

The ability to visualise the mesocollic and mesorectal lymphatics interoperatively can help surgical trainees and medical students attending the procedure in understanding the embryological planes required for effective oncological resection.

This video is itself associated with a dedicated special supplement of the journal Colorectal Disease, which contains much more detail on the system, technique and applications of near-infrared fluorescence laparoscopy in contemporary practice.
